title: Chapter 15: The Confrontation
content: The floor is empty.

Not metaphorically empty, not spiritually empty — literally, physically, every-cubicle-dark-and-every-chair-vacant empty. It's past eleven on a Thursday night, and the thirty-fourth floor of Sterling Capital belongs to exactly two people: me and the man who once made me cry in a supply closet on my fourth day of employment.
Funny how life works.

The fluorescent strips overhead hum their graveyard-shift hymn, that low electric drone that settles into your skull after enough late nights. I've stopped hearing it. What I haven't stopped noticing is the way Julian Vance's pen taps against his desk — three beats, pause, three beats, pause — like a metronome counting down to something neither of us is ready for.

We've been at this for a week. The Meridian leak case — some anonymous tip that proprietary data was walking out the door through channels nobody could trace. Garrett handed it to Julian, and Julian, in a move that confused every single person on this floor including me, pulled me onto the team. No explanation. No announcement. Just a folder slid onto my desk one morning with a Post-it note in his angular handwriting: *Start with the email headers.*

So I did. And somehow, over seven days of late nights and takeout containers and the kind of focused, combative collaboration that feels more like sparring than teamwork, we've built something that looks almost like trust.
Almost.

I bring him intel he can't trace — patterns I've spotted because I've seen this scenario play out in three different timelines, though he doesn't know that. He gives me access I haven't earned — secure databases, internal communications, doors that would stay locked if I were anyone else's second. We orbit each other like binary stars: close enough to burn, too proud to collide.

Tonight, the air conditioning has been cut to save power after hours, and the air sits heavy and warm against my skin. I can smell the stale coffee from my third cup, the faint trace of cedar and vetiver from Julian's cologne that persists even across the divide between our desks, and underneath it all, the recycled-building smell of carpet cleaner and electrical heat that is the signature scent of every office after dark.

My eyes burn. My back aches from the chair I've been in since six. But the data on my screen keeps pulling me back — email timestamps, server logs, access badges — a constellation of information that means something if I can just find the right line to connect.
Julian finds it first.
"Chen." His voice cuts through the quiet, and there's something in it I haven't heard before — not command, not challenge, but something closer to wonder. "Come look at this."

I push back from my desk. The chair wheels scrape against the low-pile carpet as I cross the narrow aisle to his station. His screen glows blue in the dark office, the only real light besides the faint wash from the emergency exit signs and my own monitor behind me. He's got the email metadata pulled into a visualization — send times plotted on a scatter graph. I lean in, my shoulder brushing the back of his chair, and I see it.
A pattern.

The leaks always happen within a forty-minute window on Tuesday afternoons. And the access logs for that window, cross-referenced with badge swipes, narrow the field to one floor. One department. Three names — and two of them were on vacation during the third incident.

"Roderick Kemp," I say. Senior VP of Strategic Partnerships. Sixty-two years old, corner office, golf handicap that gets mentioned in company newsletters. The kind of man who thinks he's untouchable because he's been touching everything for decades.

Julian nods slowly. His pen has stopped tapping. "He's been accessing files outside his division at regular intervals. Always Tuesday. Always during the all-hands meeting when half the floor is occupied and nobody's watching the servers."
"That's not a leak. That's a system."
"Exactly."

He's brilliant. Not the cold, calculating kind of brilliant I assumed he was when I first met him — the kind that dissects people like spreadsheets and finds the weaknesses in their formulas. This is different. This is the real thing. Earned analysis, actual insight, the kind of mind that sees structure in chaos because it genuinely understands how chaos works.
Not multiverse cheating. No timeline-hopping. No borrowing answers from a version of me who already solved this puzzle. Just Julian Vance, sitting in the dark, connecting dots that were always there.

I forget, for a moment, that this is the same man who made me cry in my first week. Who looked at my first project deliverable and said, "Is this what you consider adequate?" with such clinical precision that the words went straight past my defenses and into the soft tissue underneath. Who I've spent months regarding as an obstacle, a threat, a problem to navigate.
Right now, he's just... impressive.

The realization lands like a stone in my chest, and I don't know what to do with it. I've been so busy protecting myself from him that I never stopped to consider what it might mean to be on the same side. To watch him work and feel something other than defensive.
It makes me uncomfortable. More than uncomfortable — it makes me feel exposed, like I've been caught without armor I didn't even know I was wearing.
He catches me staring.
"What?" Not defensive — amused. The corner of his mouth twitches, just barely, like he's allowing himself the first fraction of an expression but hasn't decided if he'll commit to it.
I scramble. "Nothing. Just — you're good at this."

The words fall out of me without strategy or calculation, and the moment they're in the air I want to grab them back because they sound like something a person says when they're flirting, and I am not flirting with Julian Vance, I am *observing* him, there's a difference—

A pause. The silence stretches taut between us. The fluorescent hum fills it. His pen is still. His eyes are on mine, and for once the analytic scan is gone, replaced by something I can't name — something that makes the air between us feel thicker, denser, like the atmosphere before a storm decides whether to break.
"You're better."

Three syllables. Two words. The most generous thing he's ever said to me, delivered with the same even tone he uses to discuss quarterly projections, like it's simply a fact and not a grenade lobbed into the center of everything I thought I understood about us.
I don't know what to do with it. My deflection mechanism kicks in before I can stop it — before I can sit with the weight of what he just said and what it might mean that he said it.
"Don't tell anyone," I hear myself say. "I have a reputation for mediocrity to maintain."
His mouth twitches again. Higher this time. The muscles around his eyes soften by a degree that, on anyone else, might constitute a smile. On Julian Vance, it's practically a standing ovation.
He almost smiles. Almost.

And then the almost-smile fades, and something shifts in his face — a recalibration, like he's switching frequencies from the channel where we trade quips to something lower, more serious, more dangerous. He puts his pen down. The small sound of it against the desk is shockingly loud in the empty floor.
He looks at me directly.

Not the analytical scan — not the sweep that catalogues posture and tone and micro-expressions and files them under useful data. This is rawer. Unfiltered. Like he's decided to stop reading me and actually *see* me, and the difference is seismic.
"I've been wanting to ask you something," he says, "and I can't figure out how to do it without sounding like I'm interrogating you."
My shoulders tense. It's involuntary — the bracing reflex that fires every time he says something that sounds like the prelude to criticism. Old habits, carved deep.

He continues: "That night in the office, when you said your friend thought you were having a breakdown..." He pauses. I can see him choosing his words, discarding the clinical ones, reaching for something he doesn't often use. "Was that about you?"
The question lands somewhere behind my sternum and stays there, pulsing.

Nobody has asked me that. Not Mika, who texts concern in lowercase and lets me change the subject. Not my mother, who asks if I'm eating and sleeping and wearing sunscreen. Not any of the coworkers who've watched me nosebleed on documents and assumed allergies.
Not "are you performing well." Not "is the work getting done." Not "how are you managing your workload" or any of the other corporate translations of "are you functional enough to keep producing."
"Are you okay."

Just — are you okay. Asked by the last person I expected to care about the answer. Asked by the man I've spent months treating as an adversary. Asked by someone who, for all his frost and precision, just looked at me without the analytic filter and decided to ask anyway.
I break.

Not dramatically — there's no cinematic collapse, no tears tracking down my face, no crumpling against the desk. But my jaw tightens until my molars ache, and my hands find the edge of the desk and grip it like the floor might give way, my fingers pressing into the hard lip of it hard enough that I can feel the cool pressed wood through the skin.
"I don't know how to answer that honestly," I say, "without explaining things that don't make sense."

It's the closest I've come to the truth in any timeline. In timeline B, I told him I was fine and walked away. In timeline C, I deflected with a joke about caffeine and kept the mask welded in place. In timeline D, I wasn't even working this case — I was already gone, swallowed by the glitch before I could get this far.

But here, now, in this version of the story, I tell him the thing that's truest: that the reason I can't answer *is* the answer itself. That the explanation is the thing I can't explain. That the truth doesn't make sense, not in any framework he has, not in any world that runs on the rules he knows.
Julian stands.

He moves around the desk — not fast, not slow, just deliberate, the way he does everything. His footsteps are quiet on the carpet. I can hear my own breathing, too shallow, too controlled. He stops in front of me. Close. Close enough that I can see the fine weave of his shirt, the top button undone, the thin chain of a necklace I've never noticed before. Close enough that the cedar of his cologne wraps around me, warm and woody, mixing with something underneath that's just *him* — clean skin and faint traces of the black coffee he switched to three hours ago.

Close enough that I can see his face in detail I've never allowed myself to notice. The fan of faint lines at the corners of his eyes. The shadow along his jaw where he'd need to shave again by morning. The slight asymmetry of his eyebrows — the left one, arched just a fraction higher than the right, like a permanent expression of skeptical interest.
And then—
A scar.

A thin, pale line bisecting his left eyebrow, right at the arch. I see it clearly, vividly, the skin slightly raised where the tissue healed unevenly. It's there. Unmistakable. The exact same scar that Julian C has — the one from a childhood fall that he told me about in another world, another life, another timeline where we sat on a fire escape at two in the morning and he traced the scar with his fingertip while the city hummed below us.
Julian A doesn't have that scar.

He's never had it. I would have noticed — I notice everything about faces, it's a survival skill at this point, and his face has been burned into my memory whether I wanted it there or not. His left eyebrow is smooth. Unmarked. I've looked at it a hundred times.
But for a fraction of a second — a half-blink, the space between one heartbeat and the next — I *saw* it. As clear as the desk under my fingers, as real as the blood in my veins.
The glitch. It's getting worse.

My breath catches. My grip on the desk tightens. For one terrible, vertiginous moment, I don't know which Julian is standing in front of me — or if the question even makes sense anymore, if the walls between timelines are eroding so badly that a scar can migrate from one version of a man to another like a misdelivered letter.

Then it's gone. His eyebrow is smooth again. Unmarked. As if it was never any different, as if my brain invented it from whole cloth, and maybe it did, maybe I'm losing my grip on the boundary between what's real and what I remember from other lives, maybe this is what losing your mind actually feels like—
He reaches out.

His hand moves toward my face, and time slows the way it does in the moments that matter most — the way it did the first time I glitched, the way it does when a timeline collapses, the way it does when something irreversible is about to happen. I think he's going to touch my chin. Tilt my face up. Something intimate and deliberate and terrifying.
Instead, his thumb brushes my temple. Gentle. Almost clinical. But not clinical at all.
"You have blood," he says quietly. "Your nose."

My hand lifts before I can think about it. My fingers touch my upper lip — wet. Warm. Copper on my fingertips, spreading. Red on my skin. I didn't even feel it start. Another nosebleed, arriving without announcement, without the warning pressure I've learned to watch for. My body is leaking reality and I didn't notice because I was too busy looking at him.

He pulls a handkerchief from his pocket — an actual handkerchief, white cotton with a subtle border of navy stitching, pressed and folded like he's been carrying it since the nineteenth century and it still looks brand new. He holds it out to me between two fingers.
I reach for it. Our fingers brush.

The contact is a live wire. His skin against mine — the pad of his index finger grazing across my knuckle, barely a second of contact, barely anything at all — and every nerve ending in my body lights up like I've grabbed a circuit. The cotton of the handkerchief is soft and cool between my fingers, but my skin is burning where he touched it. I can feel the heat of his body from inches away, the warmth radiating off him in the air-conditioned chill.
I look up.
He's looking down.

And the space between us — the eighteen inches of recycled office air and cedar cologne and the ghost of a scar that shouldn't exist — is charged. Charged with every word we haven't said and every question we haven't answered. Charged with the fact that he asked if I was okay when no one else has. Charged with the fact that I'm standing here with blood on my lip and a handkerchief in my hand and the terrifying, exhilarating possibility pressing against the walls of my chest that he might actually see me — not the performance, not the competence, not the careful architecture of a woman who has her shit together — but the real me, the one who's glitching through fate and bleeding from the stress of it and losing her mind one timeline at a time.
If I let him.

I press the handkerchief to my lip. The cotton is absorbent and clean, and I can taste copper on my tongue beneath it. I can feel the fibers against the raw skin of my upper lip, the slight scratch of the navy stitching at the corner of my mouth.
"Thank you."
"Avery."

My name. My first name. He's never — not once, in any meeting or email or terse exchange across a desk, has he called me anything but Chen. It's been a professional barrier, a wall of formality that kept the distance between us measurable and safe.
Now he says it like it costs him something. Like he's been holding it behind his teeth for longer than he wants to admit. Like it's the most dangerous word he knows.
"Whatever it is — whatever you're not telling me —" He stops. Swallows. I watch his throat move, and the intimacy of noticing that detail is almost unbearable. "You don't have to carry it alone."

The words hang in the air between us, and I want them so badly it physically hurts — the offer, the openness, the first hand extended across the no-man's-land we've built between us since my first week. I want to take it. I want to tell him everything: the other timelines, the versions of him I've known, the glitch that's rewriting my reality, the scar I saw flicker across his face like a signal from a world he doesn't know exists. I want to say his name the way he just said mine — not as a weapon or a defense but as a doorway, an invitation, a beginning.
But I can't.

Because the last time I told someone the truth, in a timeline that no longer exists, it destroyed everything. Because the glitch takes things from me every time I get close to something real. Because the nosebleed on my lip is proof that my body is already paying a price I don't fully understand, and I don't know what happens if I add the weight of honesty to the load I'm already carrying.
Because if I tell him and he looks at me like I'm broken — like I'm the breakdown he suspected I was having — I won't survive it. Not from him.

So I hold his handkerchief against my bleeding face and look up at this man who is simultaneously the most infuriating and the most achingly kind person I have ever worked with, and I say nothing, and the silence between us is the loudest thing in the room — louder than the fluorescent hum, louder than my pulse, louder than the thundering question I'm too afraid to ask:
*What would happen if I let you in?*
And somewhere in the architecture of reality, a wall I can't see develops a crack.
